The question is just how the one God has revealed himself, and the Son praying to the Father is exactly what we'd expect if the Father and Son are distinct persons who share one divine nature.
cards.deeper: Steelman it: JWs are guarding monotheism (Deut 6:4) and find 'three in one' to look like contradiction or borrowed Greek philosophy. The Trinity is not three Gods nor one person in three modes, but one Being eternally existing as three distinct persons, Father, Son, and Spirit. The Son's prayers show distinction of persons, not inferiority of nature; John 17:5 has the Son asking for the glory he shared with the Father before the world existed, which a creature could never possess. Matthew 28:19 places the three under a single 'name.'
cards.watchOut: Never say '1+1+1=3.' The math is 1x1x1=1, three persons, one essence. Don't overclaim that you can fully comprehend it.

cards.front: Thomas saying 'My Lord and my God' was just an exclamation of surprise, like saying 'Oh my God!' He wasn't calling Jesus God.
cards.context: A JW on John 20:28:
cards.shortAnswer: That's a thoughtful read, but the text says Thomas said it directly 'to him,' to Jesus, and Jesus accepts it and blesses him for believing. If it were taking God's name in vain, wouldn't Jesus, who kept the Law perfectly, have corrected him?
cards.deeper: The 'exclamation' reading struggles because John explicitly says Thomas spoke these words 'to him' (Jesus), and Jesus responds by commending his belief rather than rebuking blasphemy. Compare Revelation 22:8-9, where an angel refuses worship that belongs to God alone, holy beings in Scripture deflect misdirected worship. Jesus receives it. The NWT itself keeps 'My Lord and my God!' here, making this a strong text to read straight from their own Bible.
cards.watchOut: Let the text and Jesus' non-correction do the work. Don't lecture; ask why Jesus didn't object.

cards.front: Colossians 2:9 says the fullness dwells in him, but that's God's qualities or purpose, not that Jesus is God himself.
cards.context: A JW on Colossians 2:9:
cards.shortAnswer: It's a striking verse, isn't it, the whole fullness of deity living in Christ bodily. If only God's qualities dwelt in him, that would describe a faithful person; but Paul says the fullness of deity, the very nature of God, lives in him in bodily form. What kind of creature could that be true of?
cards.deeper: Paul uses theotes (deity, the state of being God), not merely theiotes (divine qualities). 'Bodily' anchors it in the incarnate Christ, not an abstraction. Read it alongside Colossians 1:16-17, where all things were created in, through, and for him, and he holds all things together, language the OT reserves for Jehovah. The cumulative case is that the same person who is the image and agent of creation is the one in whom God's fullness dwells.
cards.watchOut: Don't isolate one verse. JWs expect a one-text knockout; build a pattern across Colossians.

cards.front: Before Abraham, Jesus said 'I have been,' meaning he existed first as a created spirit. He never claimed to be the 'I AM' of Exodus.
cards.context: A JW on John 8:58:
cards.shortAnswer: I hear you, and the timing is fascinating either way, Jesus existed before Abraham, who lived two thousand years earlier. But notice the crowd's reaction: they picked up stones to kill him. People don't stone you for bad grammar, they stoned him for what they understood as a claim to be God.
cards.deeper: The NWT renders ego eimi as 'I have been' to avoid the echo of Exodus 3:14 ('I AM'). But the strongest evidence is contextual: John 8:59 records the crowd immediately attempting to stone him, the prescribed penalty for blasphemy. The reaction interprets the claim. Even on the JW reading, Jesus claims conscious personal existence before Abraham, which already presses far beyond an ordinary creature and toward eternal self-existence.
cards.watchOut: Avoid hinging everything on Greek tense. The stoning reaction is the clincher even granting their translation.

cards.front: The Holy Spirit is God's active force, like electricity. It's a power, not a person.
cards.context: A JW on the Holy Spirit:
cards.shortAnswer: That's the official explanation, I know. But the Bible says the Spirit can be lied to, grieved, and speaks and decides things, and an impersonal force can't be lied to or grieved. Can you grieve electricity?
cards.deeper: Steelman: JWs note the Spirit is sometimes described in impersonal imagery (poured out, filled). But Scripture attributes personal acts and attributes to the Spirit, in Acts 5:3-4 lying to the Spirit is equated with lying to God; the Spirit can be grieved (Eph 4:30), speaks and commissions (Acts 13:2), and teaches and intercedes. These are properties of a person, not a force. Acts 5 also directly identifies the Spirit as God.
cards.watchOut: Don't mock 'electricity.' Ask the diagnostic question and let them feel the weight.

cards.front: Jesus is Michael the archangel. The 'voice of the archangel' at the resurrection and Michael fighting the dragon show this.
cards.context: A JW on Jesus as Michael:
cards.shortAnswer: Can I show you the very next chapter from the same writer? Hebrews 1 asks, 'to which of the angels did God ever say, You are my Son?' and then commands the angels to worship the Son. If Jesus is an angel, Hebrews 1 doesn't make sense.
cards.deeper: The Michael identification rests on inference (1 Thess 4:16's 'archangel's voice,' Jude 9, Daniel/Revelation battle scenes), not a direct statement. Hebrews 1 systematically contrasts the Son with angels: no angel is called God's Son in this way, and verse 6 commands all angels to worship him, worship an angel must refuse (Rev 22:8-9). Verse 8 has the Father addressing the Son as 'God.' The author's whole point is the Son's superiority to and difference in kind from angels.
cards.watchOut: Keep it in Hebrews 1, one clean chapter. Don't chase every Michael proof-text down a rabbit trail.

cards.front: Only 144,000 go to heaven to rule with Christ. The rest of us, the 'other sheep,' have an earthly hope in paradise. Two groups, two hopes.
cards.context: A JW on the 144,000 and other sheep:
cards.shortAnswer: Help me understand something, because I want to get your view right. Jesus said his 'other sheep' would become 'one flock' with one shepherd, so where does Scripture say the two hopes never share the same destiny? And does the new covenant in his blood apply to you personally?
cards.deeper: Steelman: JWs read Revelation 7's 144,000 (a literal sealed number) as distinct from the 'great crowd,' and John 10:16 as two folds. But John 10:16 actually says the two become 'one flock, one shepherd,' unity, not a permanent two-tier system. Revelation 7:9 shows a great multitude no one could number before the throne. The pastoral pressure point: most JWs do not partake of the bread and cup at their annual Memorial because they don't count themselves among the 144,000, which means they are taught the new covenant in Christ's blood (Luke 22:20) is not for them, the gospel's central promise held at arm's length.
cards.watchOut: This is tender. Many JWs feel second-class spiritually. Be gentle; the goal is to show them the cup is for them.

cards.front: We won't accept blood transfusions because God commands us to abstain from blood. We take the Bible seriously even when it's costly.
cards.context: A JW on blood transfusions:
cards.shortAnswer: I genuinely admire that you'd obey God even when it's hard, that's real conviction. The texts about 'abstaining from blood' are about eating it, the diet of pagan altars; can we look at whether a life-saving transfusion is the same thing as eating blood?
cards.deeper: Honor the costliness, this is sincere obedience, not stubbornness. The biblical commands (Gen 9, Lev 17, Acts 15) concern eating/consuming blood in a sacrificial-dietary context, not intravenous medical treatment, which did not exist. The deeper concern is that the prohibition has become a test of organizational loyalty with life-and-death stakes, and that mercy toward life is itself a biblical principle (Mark 7, Matthew 12:7). This is rarely the door-opening issue; address Jesus' identity first.
cards.watchOut: Do not lead with this, it can feel like an attack on family members who've died. Affirm their sincerity first.

cards.front: Salvation isn't just believing. You have to do God's will, preach, and stay faithful to Jehovah's organization to survive Armageddon.
cards.context: A JW on salvation by works and the organization:
cards.shortAnswer: Can I ask you honestly, do you have assurance right now that you're saved, or is it always 'we'll see at Armageddon'? Because the Bible says we can know we have eternal life as a present gift, paid in full by Christ, not earned by our record.
cards.deeper: Steelman: JWs rightly reject 'cheap grace' and cite James that faith without works is dead. But the JW system makes final salvation contingent on works, door-to-door ministry, and faithfulness to the organization, producing chronic uncertainty (always 'survive Armageddon,' never settled). Scripture teaches salvation by grace through faith, not works (Eph 2:8-9; Titus 3:5), and that believers may KNOW they have eternal life now (1 John 5:13). Works are the fruit of salvation, not the price of it. The contrast between a present, assured gift and a lifelong probation is one of the most liberating things you can offer.
cards.watchOut: Distinguish grace-as-the-root from works-as-the-fruit so they don't hear 'obedience doesn't matter.'

cards.front: Jehovah's organization is God's channel of truth today. We need the faithful and discerning slave to understand the Bible correctly.
cards.context: A JW on the Watchtower's authority:
cards.shortAnswer: I understand wanting a trustworthy guide, we all want to be sure we're not deceived. But the Bible itself tells us to test every teaching against Scripture, like the Bereans did. Are we allowed to check the organization's teachings against the Bible, or is that off-limits?
cards.deeper: Steelman: humans crave a reliable interpreter, and JWs sincerely believe the Governing Body protects them from error and division. But the same Scripture they revere commands believers to test teachings (Acts 17:11; 1 John 4:1) and warns that even an angel preaching another gospel is accursed (Gal 1:8). If the final authority is an organization that cannot be questioned, then Scripture is no longer supreme. Gently establish the principle that the Bible judges every teacher, before examining any specific claim.
cards.watchOut: Don't attack the organization head-on early, it triggers loyalty defenses. Establish the testing principle from their own Bible first.

cards.front: The Watchtower has correctly understood Bible prophecy. We don't follow date-setters; we follow God's Word.
cards.context: A JW on the Watchtower's prophetic record:
cards.shortAnswer: I'm not interested in scoring points, but the Bible gives us a clear test in Deuteronomy: if someone speaks in God's name and it doesn't come true, that word wasn't from him. Would you be willing to look at whether dates the organization taught about the 'last days' actually came to pass?
cards.deeper: Steelman: the organization frames past expectations as eager but imperfect human understanding, refined over time, not false prophecy. But Deuteronomy 18:21-22 gives a sobering standard for anyone claiming to speak God's mind. The pastoral aim is not humiliation but to loosen the assumption that the channel is infallible, so the person feels free to weigh teachings against Scripture. Always cite specifics accurately and verifiably, or not at all; an overstated charge destroys your credibility and your gentleness.
cards.watchOut: Only cite dates/claims you can verify. A single fabricated or exaggerated detail forfeits trust, and the relationship.

cards.front: True worshipers must use God's personal name, Jehovah. Christendom hid it. We've restored it, even in the New Testament.
cards.context: A JW on the divine name:
cards.shortAnswer: I love that you honor God's name, that's a beautiful instinct, and the name matters. Here's something worth noticing together: in the New Testament the inspired writers quote Old Testament 'Jehovah' verses and apply them to Jesus. Can I show you one?
cards.deeper: Steelman: reverence for the divine name (YHWH) is genuinely biblical, and JWs are reacting against centuries of substituting 'LORD.' The pivot is that the NT applies OT YHWH texts directly to Jesus, Joel 2:32 ('everyone who calls on the name of Jehovah will be saved') is quoted in Romans 10:13 and applied to calling on Jesus as Lord. Philippians 2:9-11 gives Jesus 'the name above every name' and every knee bowing, echoing Isaiah 45:23, a YHWH text. The very name they exalt, the NT attaches to the Son.
cards.watchOut: Note the NWT inserts 'Jehovah' into the NT where the Greek has 'Lord,' which conveniently obscures these Jesus-applications. Mention it gently, not as a gotcha.

cards.front: Jesus didn't die on a cross. He died on a single upright stake. The cross is a pagan symbol Christendom adopted.
cards.context: A JW on the 'torture stake':
cards.shortAnswer: Honestly, the shape of the wood isn't where I'd want to spend our time, because whether it was a cross or a stake, what saves us is that he died in our place and rose again. Can we talk about that, what his death actually accomplished for you and me?
cards.deeper: Steelman: the Greek stauros can mean an upright pole, and some pagan symbols predate Christianity, so JWs see the cross as a corruption. This is genuinely a secondary issue, John 20:25 mentions 'nails' (plural) in his hands, which some argue fits a crossbar, but the gospel does not hinge on the geometry. Refuse to let this become the main event; redirect to the meaning of the atonement (1 Cor 1:18; 1 Peter 2:24). Spending an hour on stake-versus-cross is exactly the kind of detour that wastes a gospel conversation.
cards.watchOut: Don't take the bait. This is a distraction from Christ's identity and work; acknowledge and redirect.

cards.front: We don't celebrate birthdays or holidays like Christmas and Easter because they have pagan roots and aren't commanded in the Bible.
cards.context: A JW on holidays and birthdays:
cards.shortAnswer: I respect wanting your worship to be pure and not borrowed from idolatry, that's a good motive. I'd just gently ask: Romans 14 says believers can differ on special days and not judge each other, so could this be a matter of conscience rather than a salvation test?
cards.deeper: Steelman: JWs want to avoid syncretism and note the two negative birthday accounts in Scripture (Pharaoh, Herod) and pagan associations with some holidays. Paul, however, explicitly treats the observance of special days as a conscience matter where believers should not judge one another (Rom 14:5-6; Col 2:16-17), and warns against bondage to a calendar of required days (Gal 4:9-10). The concern is that abstaining has become a boundary marker of group loyalty rather than a freedom of conscience. This is minor; don't lead with it.
cards.watchOut: Low-priority issue. Mentioning it can sound like you just want to defend Christmas. Keep the focus on Christ.

cards.front: Why do we go door to door? Because Jesus commanded us to preach the good news, and faithful witnessing is part of our salvation.
cards.context: A JW explaining the ministry:
cards.shortAnswer: I actually find that convicting, you're out here doing what a lot of Christians stay home and skip. Can I ask what the good news is that you're carrying? When you knock on a door, what's the core message you most want someone to hear?
cards.deeper: Steelman: their evangelistic zeal genuinely outpaces most professing Christians, and the Great Commission really does command witnessing. The gentle probe is to ask them to articulate 'the good news,' which often surfaces a message centered on Kingdom government and works/faithfulness rather than Christ crucified and risen for sinners (1 Cor 15:3-4). The contrast between a gospel you must complete by your record and a gospel already finished by Christ becomes visible when they state their message in their own words.
cards.watchOut: Affirm the zeal sincerely, then ask them to define the gospel. Listen more than you talk here.

cards.front: How could I ever use your own Bible to talk about Jesus without it turning into an argument?
cards.context: Joshua preparing his approach:
cards.shortAnswer: Open the NWT to texts it renders in your favor and ask honest questions: Hebrews 1:6 (angels worship the Son), John 20:28 ('My Lord and my God'), Colossians 2:9 ('fullness of the divine quality bodily'), and Philippians 2:10-11. Let the verses and a calm 'what do you make of that?' do the work.
cards.deeper: This is a prep card for tactics, not a single objection. The NWT, even with its renderings, still contains texts that press toward Christ's deity: angels worshiping the Son (Heb 1:6), Thomas's confession (John 20:28), the fullness dwelling bodily (Col 2:9), and every knee bowing and tongue confessing Jesus as Lord (Phil 2:10-11, echoing Isaiah 45:23). The Koukl/Columbo approach, asking 'what do you mean by that?' and 'how did you come to that conclusion?', keeps you in the seat of a curious friend rather than an opponent, and lets the Spirit use Scripture itself. Pray before, during, and after; conversion is God's work, not your cleverness.
cards.watchOut: Aim for many gracious conversations over time, not one knockout. Plant seeds, leave the door open, and pray.

cards.front: Before we even start — I just want you to know I respect that you came to my door. That takes real courage and conviction. Can I ask what your faith means to you personally?
cards.context: Opening posture, not an objection:
cards.shortAnswer: I genuinely admire your devotion — the family commitment, the missions, the discipline. Before I say anything, I'd love to actually hear what YOU believe in your own words, not the version I might have heard secondhand.
cards.deeper: LDS missionaries and members are often braced for hostility or for evangelicals who attack a caricature. The single most disarming move is sincere honor of their sincerity, followed by genuine curiosity. Steelman: Latter-day Saints are typically devout, family-centered, morally serious, and self-sacrificing (two-year missions at their own expense). Beginning with respect and a question earns the right to be heard and surfaces what THIS person actually believes, since LDS belief varies and has shifted over time.
cards.watchOut: Don't open with doctrine or 'gotchas.' Win the person first; ask before you assert.

cards.front: We're Christians too — we believe in Jesus Christ. It's right there in our name, The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. Why do you say we're not?
cards.context: An LDS member says:
cards.shortAnswer: I hear you, and I don't doubt you love Jesus sincerely. The honest question between us isn't whether we both say 'Jesus,' but whether we mean the same Jesus — so can I ask you what you believe about who He is and where He came from?
cards.deeper: Steelman: LDS members genuinely center Christ in worship and devotion and find it hurtful to be told they aren't Christian. The real issue is definitional, not tribal. Historic Christianity confesses one eternal, uncreated God who is Father, Son, and Spirit; LDS theology teaches God the Father was once a man who progressed to godhood, and Jesus is His firstborn spirit-child. Paul warns that 'another Jesus' and 'a different gospel' can be preached under familiar words (2 Corinthians 11:4). Focus the conversation on the identity of God and Christ rather than on the label.
cards.watchOut: Don't lead with 'you're not Christian' — it ends the conversation. Lead with 'help me understand who your Jesus is.'

cards.front: God the Father was once a man like us, who lived a righteous life and progressed to become God. As Lorenzo Snow put it, 'As man now is, God once was; as God now is, man may be.' Isn't that an amazing hope?
cards.context: An LDS member explains eternal progression:
cards.shortAnswer: I can see why that's compelling — the idea that we could become like our Father. But the Bible says God has always been God, with no one before or after Him; He's not a graduated man but eternal by nature. Can I show you a couple of verses and hear how you read them?
cards.deeper: Steelman: Eternal progression offers a deeply hopeful, relational vision — God as a loving Father we can ultimately resemble — and it takes seriously verses about being 'children of God' and 'partakers of the divine nature.' The biblical response: Isaiah 43:10 says 'before me no god was formed, nor shall there be any after me,' which directly excludes a chain of progressing gods. Psalm 90:2 grounds God's deity from everlasting to everlasting, and Malachi 3:6 / James 1:17 affirm He does not change. 'Partakers of the divine nature' (2 Peter 1:4) means moral conformity to Christ, not becoming ontological gods. The Creator/creature distinction is non-negotiable in Scripture.
cards.watchOut: Note that LDS leaders today emphasize this less publicly; ask if HE affirms it rather than assuming.

cards.front: We believe in God the Father, His Son Jesus Christ, and the Holy Ghost — three separate beings, perfectly one in purpose. Isn't 'three persons, one being' just confusing Greek philosophy the Bible never actually teaches?
cards.context: An LDS member contrasts the Godhead with the Trinity:
cards.shortAnswer: That's a fair challenge — the Trinity isn't a tidy equation, I'll grant that. But the Bible insists on two things at once: there is only one God, and the Father, Son, and Spirit are each fully that one God. Could I walk through where Scripture says God is numerically one?
cards.deeper: Steelman: The LDS 'social' Godhead (three distinct beings united in purpose) sidesteps apparent paradox and reads naturally onto passages where the Father and Son interact. The biblical response: Scripture is relentlessly monotheistic — 'the LORD is one' (Deuteronomy 6:4), 'there is no God besides me' (Isaiah 44:6), 'besides me there is no god' (Isaiah 45:5). Three separate gods 'one in purpose' is tritheism, not biblical monotheism. The Trinity isn't 'three Gods' or 'one person'; it's one divine Being eternally existing as three persons. The doctrine is the church's attempt to hold together all the data Scripture forces on us, not imported philosophy.
cards.watchOut: Don't overclaim that the word 'Trinity' is in the Bible — defend the concept from the data, gently.

cards.front: Jesus is our Elder Brother — the firstborn of the Father's spirit children. We're all spirit children of God who lived with Him before this life. Doesn't that make Him beautifully relatable to us?
cards.context: An LDS member describes Jesus' origin:
cards.shortAnswer: I love that you treasure Jesus' nearness to us — and He truly is our brother in His humanity. But John says the Word WAS God and made everything that exists, so He can't be a created spirit-child; He's the eternal Creator who became one of us. Want to look at John 1 together?
cards.deeper: Steelman: 'Elder Brother' Christology emphasizes Christ's solidarity with us and His role as the pattern of exaltation, and it draws on 'firstborn' language (Colossians 1:15). The biblical response: 'firstborn' in Colossians 1:15 means preeminent heir, not first-created — because verse 16 says ALL things were created by Him and FOR Him, and verse 17 says He is before all things. John 1:1-3 and John 8:58 ('before Abraham was, I AM') affirm His eternal, uncreated deity. A created being cannot be the Creator of everything; Jesus is not the first creature but the eternal Son.
cards.watchOut: Affirm His true brotherhood in the incarnation before correcting the 'spirit-child' framing.

cards.front: In our understanding, Jesus and Lucifer were both spirit children of God in the premortal life. Lucifer rebelled and Jesus' plan was chosen. Why does that bother evangelicals so much?
cards.context: An LDS member explains the premortal council:
cards.shortAnswer: It bothers me because it puts the eternal Creator and a created angel on the same shelf as siblings — but Scripture says Jesus made the angels, including the one who fell, so He's their Maker, not their brother. Can I ask where you first learned the premortal-council teaching?
cards.deeper: Steelman: The premortal council is a coherent narrative in LDS cosmology explaining the origin of agency, the fall, and Christ's volunteering to be Savior; many members find it gives the spiritual war real stakes. The biblical response: Hebrews 1 explicitly contrasts the Son with angels — He is 'much superior to the angels,' worshiped BY them, and addressed as God (Hebrews 1:6-8). Colossians 1:16 says thrones, dominions, rulers, and authorities (including angelic beings) were created through Christ. Satan is a created, fallen cherub (Ezekiel 28); equating him as Jesus' literal spirit-brother collapses the infinite gap between Creator and creature.
cards.watchOut: This can feel like a cheap shot if blurted ('your church says Jesus is Satan's brother'). Handle it gently and accurately, as cosmology, not insult.

cards.front: We absolutely believe in grace. The Book of Mormon says, 'It is by grace that we are saved, after all we can do.' Grace and effort work together — what's wrong with that?
cards.context: An LDS member cites 2 Nephi 25:23:
cards.shortAnswer: I'm so glad grace matters to you — it's the heart of everything. The one phrase I'd gently push on is 'after all we can do,' because if salvation kicks in only after I've maxed out my effort, how would I ever know I've done enough? Scripture says it's a gift, not a wage.
cards.deeper: Steelman: 2 Nephi 25:23 and the LDS emphasis on covenant-keeping reflect a serious moral earnestness and a refusal of cheap grace; they read James 2 ('faith without works is dead') as proof that grace and obedience cooperate. The biblical response: Ephesians 2:8-9 grounds salvation in grace through faith, 'not of works, so that no one may boast,' and Romans 11:6 insists grace and works are mutually exclusive as the BASIS of salvation ('if by grace, then it is no longer on the basis of works'). Christ's righteousness is imputed to us by faith (2 Corinthians 5:21). Works are the fruit and evidence of salvation (James), never the price of it. 'After all we can do' subtly makes grace the leftover gap-filler rather than the whole foundation.
cards.watchOut: Don't dismiss works entirely — affirm that real faith works (James). The issue is basis vs. fruit, not whether obedience matters.

cards.front: How can you say I'm not saved by grace? I'm trying my best to keep the commandments, serve, and be worthy. Doesn't God expect us to be worthy?
cards.context: An LDS member on worthiness:
cards.shortAnswer: Here's the freeing news: the gospel isn't 'be worthy enough,' it's that Jesus was worthy in your place and gives you His record as a gift. Can I ask — do you ever feel the weight of wondering if you've measured up? That weight is exactly what grace lifts.
cards.deeper: Steelman: 'Worthiness' in LDS practice (temple recommends, ordinances) reflects a sincere desire to honor God and a high view of holiness. The biblical response: Romans 4:4-5 says to the one who does NOT work but trusts God who justifies the ungodly, faith is credited as righteousness. Philippians 3:9 contrasts 'a righteousness of my own' with the righteousness from God that depends on faith. 2 Corinthians 5:21 is the great exchange: our sin to Christ, His righteousness to us. The pastoral key is the assurance question — a works-tinged system structurally withholds assurance, which is precisely the burden the imputed righteousness of Christ removes.
cards.watchOut: Use the assurance question pastorally, not as a trap. Many LDS feel this weight privately; be tender.

cards.front: The Book of Mormon is another testament of Jesus Christ — it doesn't replace the Bible, it confirms it. Why wouldn't God give more scripture? Doesn't He still speak?
cards.context: An LDS member defends the Book of Mormon:
cards.shortAnswer: I'd never say God can't speak — He does, through His Spirit and His Word. My concern is that Paul says if anyone, even an angel, preaches a different gospel, we shouldn't receive it — so the test isn't 'more revelation,' it's whether it matches the gospel already given. Could we compare them?
cards.deeper: Steelman: The LDS argument that God is a speaking God who could give additional scripture is theologically reasonable on its face, and Latter-day Saints sincerely believe the Book of Mormon points to Christ. The biblical response: Galatians 1:8-9 makes the GOSPEL the canon-test — 'even if an angel from heaven should preach' another gospel, let him be accursed — and the LDS gospel of grace-plus-works-plus-exaltation diverges from Paul's. 2 Timothy 3:16-17 says Scripture makes the man of God 'complete, equipped for every good work.' The issue isn't God's freedom to speak but whether a later message contradicts the once-delivered gospel (Jude 3).
cards.watchOut: Don't argue archaeology of the Book of Mormon first; the gospel-content test (Galatians 1:8) is sharper and more winsome.

cards.front: We have living prophets and apostles today, just like the early church. Why would God leave us with only an ancient book and no living voice to guide us?
cards.context: An LDS member on continuing revelation:
cards.shortAnswer: I get the appeal of a living voice — but the test of any prophet has always been whether their words match God's already-revealed truth, and a true prophet is never wrong. Can I ask: how do you personally weigh a modern prophet's words against the Bible when they seem to differ?
cards.deeper: Steelman: The desire for present guidance is real, and the apostolic-succession instinct echoes the New Testament's living leadership; LDS members find security in current direction. The biblical response: Hebrews 1:1-2 marks a shift — God formerly spoke through prophets but 'in these last days has spoken to us by his Son,' the final and supreme revelation. Deuteronomy 18:21-22 gives the prophetic test: 100% accuracy. This invites honest examination of LDS prophetic predictions and reversals (e.g., shifts on priesthood and plural marriage) without mockery. Scripture's sufficiency (2 Timothy 3:16-17) means we're not left guessing.
cards.watchOut: Don't ridicule specific prophets. Ask how THEY reconcile prophet vs. Scripture; let the tension surface gently.

cards.front: After the apostles died, the true church and priesthood authority were lost — a Great Apostasy. That's why a restoration through Joseph Smith was necessary. Doesn't church history show corruption crept in?
cards.context: An LDS member explains the restoration's premise:
cards.shortAnswer: There's truth in that the church has always battled corruption — Jesus knew it would. But He promised the gates of hell would NOT prevail against His church, so if it totally vanished for centuries, His promise failed. Where do you see that promise fitting the apostasy idea?
cards.deeper: Steelman: The Great Apostasy claim honestly reckons with real medieval corruption, doctrinal drift, and abuses; it gives the Restoration its reason for being. The biblical response: a TOTAL apostasy contradicts Jesus' explicit promises — 'I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it' (Matthew 16:18), 'I am with you always, to the end of the age' (Matthew 28:20), and 'glory in the church... throughout all generations' (Ephesians 3:21). Reformation, not restoration-from-zero, is the biblical pattern; God always preserved a faithful remnant. If the gospel and authority were truly lost for 1,700 years, Christ's guarantee would have been broken.
cards.watchOut: Concede the reality of historical corruption — don't defend a perfect institutional history. The point is total disappearance vs. Christ's promise.

cards.front: I know the Book of Mormon is true because I prayed about it and felt the Spirit confirm it — a burning in my bosom. That witness is more sure to me than any argument. Haven't you felt God confirm something to you?
cards.context: An LDS member shares their testimony (Moroni 10:4):
cards.shortAnswer: I have felt deeply moved by God, so I take your experience seriously and I won't belittle it. But feelings can confirm contradictory things — sincere people of every faith feel them — so wouldn't a truly good God also give us objective evidence we can test, not just an inner feeling?
cards.deeper: Steelman: The 'burning in the bosom' (rooted in Doctrine & Covenants 9:8 and Moroni 10:4) reflects a genuine, often life-shaping spiritual experience, and Scripture does affirm the Spirit's inner witness (Romans 8:16). The biblical response: feelings are real but not self-validating — 1 John 4:1 commands us to 'test the spirits,' and Jeremiah 17:9 warns the heart is deceitful. Muslims, Hindus, and others report equally vivid confirmations of contradictory claims, so subjective feeling can't be the final arbiter. God anchored the gospel in publicly testable history — eyewitnesses, the resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:3-8), fulfilled prophecy — inviting examination (1 Thessalonians 5:21), not just sentiment.
cards.watchOut: Never mock the feeling or call it demonic. Validate the sincerity, then gently introduce the need for an external test.

cards.front: Why do you baptize for the dead? It seems strange to be baptized on behalf of people who already died.
cards.context: Asking an LDS member to explain (an evangelist's question):
cards.shortAnswer: I'd actually love to hear your reasoning first, since I know it comes from a place of love for ancestors. My hesitation is that the Bible says it's appointed for each person to die once and then face judgment — no second chance to be saved after death. How do you see those fitting together?
cards.deeper: Steelman: Proxy baptism for the dead flows from a beautiful impulse — that God's mercy might reach ancestors who never heard the gospel — and it leans on Paul's single, cryptic mention in 1 Corinthians 15:29. The biblical response: Paul mentions the practice without endorsing it (he says 'they,' not 'we'), and his point is simply to argue for the resurrection's reality. Hebrews 9:27 ('it is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment') and Jesus' parable of the fixed chasm (Luke 16:26) rule out post-mortem salvation. The hopeful motive is real; the doctrinal foundation is one ambiguous verse against clear teaching that destiny is sealed at death.
cards.watchOut: Lead with curiosity about the loving motive (ancestors) before raising Hebrews 9:27.

cards.front: Our goal is eternal families — to be sealed together forever in the celestial kingdom. Doesn't your heaven sound lonely by comparison, where marriage just ends?
cards.context: An LDS member on eternal families and degrees of glory:
cards.shortAnswer: The love behind 'families are forever' is genuinely beautiful, and we WILL be together with our loved ones in Christ forever. But Jesus said in the resurrection people neither marry nor are given in marriage — because the relationship that fills heaven is union with Him. Can I show you what He said?
cards.deeper: Steelman: The 'eternal families' and three-degrees-of-glory framework (1 Corinthians 15:40-41 read through LDS lenses) speaks to the deep human longing for enduring love and gives marriage eternal weight — a genuinely attractive vision. The biblical response: Jesus directly addressed this in Matthew 22:30 — in the resurrection 'they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like angels.' Heaven's center is God Himself dwelling with His people (Revelation 21:3-4), and we will be like Christ, seeing Him as He is (1 John 3:2). Believing families are reunited in Christ, but the exalting hope is not graded kingdoms earned by worthiness; it's the undeserved gift of seeing God face to face.
cards.watchOut: Affirm the longing for reunion — don't make heaven sound cold. Redirect to Christ as heaven's joy, not 'no families.'

cards.front: It all began with the First Vision — Joseph Smith saw God the Father and Jesus Christ as two distinct, glorified beings. That single event answers the whole question of who God is. Why won't you accept his testimony?
cards.context: An LDS member appeals to the First Vision:
cards.shortAnswer: I take Joseph's claim seriously enough to examine it carefully — and what gives me pause is that the account itself was told in several different versions over the years. If the foundational event shifted in the retelling, isn't it fair to ask which version we're trusting?
cards.deeper: Steelman: The First Vision is the cornerstone of LDS faith and members experience it as a profound, faith-anchoring reality that resolves the nature of God. The biblical and historical response: there are multiple First Vision accounts (the 1832, 1835, and canonical 1838 versions) that differ in significant details — who appeared (the Lord; angels; the Father and the Son), Joseph's age, and the purpose. Scripture establishes truth on consistent testimony (Deuteronomy 19:15; 2 Corinthians 13:1) and commands testing claimed revelation (1 John 4:1). Even granting sincerity, the developing accounts invite honest scrutiny rather than acceptance on assertion alone.
cards.watchOut: State the multiple-accounts point factually and humbly; don't accuse Joseph of lying or sneer. Let the discrepancy raise the question.

cards.front: Honestly, all this doctrine-comparing feels divisive. Can't we just agree we both love Jesus and try to be good people, and leave it there?
cards.context: An LDS member wants to de-escalate:
cards.shortAnswer: I really do value the peace you're after, and I'm not here to fight. But because I believe what's at stake is eternal, loving you means caring enough to be honest about the real Jesus — the one who saves us not by our goodness but by His. Could I just share what makes that good news so good?
cards.deeper: Steelman: The desire to avoid contentious division is itself Christlike in spirit, and nobody enjoys feeling argued at. The biblical response: truth and love are not opposites — Ephesians 4:15 calls us to speak the truth IN love. Because Jesus is the only way (John 14:6) and salvation is found in no one else (Acts 4:12), genuine love cannot settle for vague agreement when the identity of God and the basis of salvation differ. The pivot is from debate to gospel: the grace of Christ, who saves the ungodly as a free gift (Ephesians 2:8-9), is the most loving thing you can offer.
cards.watchOut: Don't 'win' by overpowering. Pivot from argument to the beauty of grace — make the gospel sound like good news, because it is.

cards.front: So if it's all just grace and faith, doesn't that give people a free pass to sin? Where's the motivation to live righteously?
cards.context: An LDS member challenges grace alone:
cards.shortAnswer: Great question — and Paul asked it too: 'Shall we sin so grace may abound? By no means!' Grace doesn't kill obedience; it gives it a new engine. We obey out of gratitude and love, not to earn what's already a gift. Doesn't being loved first actually free you to obey more joyfully?
cards.deeper: Steelman: The LDS concern that grace alone breeds license is a serious, historically recurring objection (the 'antinomian' worry), and it reflects real love for holiness. The biblical response: Paul anticipates exactly this in Romans 6:1-2 and answers that the believer has died to sin. Titus 2:11-12 says grace TRAINS us to renounce ungodliness. Ephesians 2:10 says we're saved for good works — works are the destination, not the down payment. True grace transforms the heart so obedience flows from love and gratitude, producing MORE holiness than fear-based earning ever could, because it removes the anxious self-focus and fixes the eyes on Christ.
cards.watchOut: Don't concede that grace alone is morally lax — show it's the only thing that truly produces holiness, from the inside out.

cards.front: If you could leave me with just one thing, what would you want me to know about your Jesus?
cards.context: A softened LDS member asks sincerely (use this to close):
cards.shortAnswer: That you can stop wondering if you've done enough. My Jesus already did enough — He lived the perfect life you and I couldn't, took our failure on the cross, and offers His own righteousness as a free gift you receive by trusting Him. Would you ever be willing to read the book of Romans with me and just see?
cards.deeper: Steelman: A respectful, lengthy conversation may move an LDS person from defending the institution to genuinely considering Christ; the most powerful close is not a final argument but the offer of rest. The biblical response: Romans 5:1 — 'since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God' — names the assurance a works-tinged system can't give. The great exchange (2 Corinthians 5:21) and the free gift (Ephesians 2:8-9) are the heart of the matter. Inviting them to read Romans together moves the conversation from your authority to God's Word, where the Spirit does the convincing. The aim is an ongoing relationship, not a one-time conquest.
cards.watchOut: End with an invitation and an open door, not a verdict. Offer to keep talking; leave them wanting the rest Christ gives.
